AWS announces general availability of Smithy-Java client framework

AWS today announced the general availability of Smithy-Java, an open-source Java framework for generating type-safe clients and standalone classes from Smithy models. Smithy-Java addresses one of the most consistently r...

#AWS #

external.description:
AWS today announced the general availability of Smithy-Java, an open-source Java framework for generating type-safe clients and standalone classes from Smithy models. Smithy-Java addresses one of the most consistently requested capabilities from enterprise Smithy users: production-grade Java SDK generation. The framework allows you to generate clients from models and async patterns that increase cognitive load and maintenance burden for developers building modern Java applications.

 Built on Java 21's virtual threads, Smithy-Java provides a blocking-style API that is both simpler to use and competitive in performance with complex async alternatives. Key benefits include auto-generated type-safe clients from Smithy, protocol flexibility with runtime protocol swapping for gradual migration paths. The GA release includes the Java client code generator, support for AWS SigV4 and all major AWS protocols (AWS JSON, REST-JSON, REST-XML, AWS Query, and Smithy RPCv2-CBOR), standalone type code generation for sharing types across multiple services or data modeling, and a dynamic client that can call Smithy services without a codegen step.

 The framework pioneers two architectural innovations: schema-driven serialization that reduces SDK size while improving performance, and binary decision diagrams (BDD) for endpoint rules resolution delivering significant latency improvements. Internal Amazon teams have already built complete services in weeks rather than months using Smithy-Java, with service teams depending on it internally. The framework is ideal for organizations invested in the Smithy ecosystem, teams requiring protocol-agnostic development, and developers building new services with generated server stubs.

 To learn more, visit our https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/developer/smithy-java-client-framework-is-now-generally-available/ and follow the https://github.com/smithy-lang/smithy-java.
external.title: AWS announces general availability of Smithy-Java client framework

Amazon WorkSpaces Personal now supports unique DNS names for PrivateLink

Amazon WorkSpaces Personal now provides unique, publicly resolvable Domain Name System (DNS) names for each AWS PrivateLink Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) endpoint, enabling enterprise customers to de...

#AWS #AmazonWorkspaces

external.description:
Amazon WorkSpaces Personal now provides unique, publicly resolvable Domain Name System (DNS) names for each AWS PrivateLink Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) endpoint, enabling enterprise customers to deploy WorkSpaces across multiple AWS VPCs and accounts without DNS resolution conflicts. Each interface VPC endpoint now receives a globally unique AWS-managed DNS name in addition to the previous generic DNS name that was shared across all endpoints.
 
 This enhancement enables customers to route traffic appropriately in multi-account environments with centralized DNS infrastructure. Customers can now deploy WorkSpaces Personal directories across different VPCs and AWS accounts while maintaining proper security isolation, eliminating the DNS name collision that previously prevented customers from using separate interface VPC endpoints across accounts. The publicly resolvable DNS names simplify configuration while maintaining security, as they resolve to private IP addresses accessible only from within the respective VPC. The unique DNS names are automatically managed by AWS throughout their lifecycle, requiring no additional Route 53 configuration or custom DNS management.
 
 This feature is available in all AWS https://aws.amazon.com/about-aws/global-infrastructure/regional-product-services/ where PrivateLink is available in Amazon WorkSpaces Personal.
 
 To learn more, see https://docs.aws.amazon.com/workspaces/latest/adminguide/creating-streaming-vpc-endpoints.html. For configuration details, refer to the https://docs.aws.amazon.com/workspaces/latest/adminguide/amazon-workspaces.html. Existing customers will automatically benefit from this enhancement, as the system maintains backward compatibility with previous DNS configurations.
external.title: Amazon WorkSpaces Personal now supports unique DNS names for PrivateLink

Amazon Verified Permissions now supports policy store aliases and named policies and policy templates

Today, AWS announces support for policy store aliases and named policies and policy templates in Amazon Verified Permissions, simplifying multi-tenant deployme...

#AWS #AmazonVerifiedPermissions

external.description:
Today, AWS announces support for policy store aliases and named policies and policy templates in Amazon Verified Permissions, simplifying multi-tenant deployments and day-to-day policy management. Amazon Verified Permissions is a fine-grained authorization service that helps you manage and enforce permissions across your applications using Cedar policies. These new capabilities eliminate the need to maintain separate mapping tables for associating tenant identifiers with policy store IDs or tracking individual policy and template IDs.

 With policy store aliases, multi-tenant application developers can assign a human-readable alias based on a tenant identifier and use it in any API call, removing the need for a lookup table. Similarly, named policies and policy templates let you reference policies by meaningful names instead of system-generated IDs, making it easier to manage authorization logic as your application grows.

 Amazon Verified Permissions policy store aliases and named policies and templates are available in all AWS Regions where Amazon Verified Permissions is available. For a full list of supported Regions, see https://docs.aws.amazon.com/general/latest/gr/verifiedpermissions.html.

 To get started, see https://docs.aws.amazon.com/verifiedpermissions/latest/userguide/policy-store-aliases.html and https://docs.aws.amazon.com/verifiedpermissions/latest/userguide/policies-create.html in the Amazon Verified Permissions User Guide, or visit the https://docs.aws.amazon.com/verifiedpermissions/latest/apireference/.
external.title: Amazon Verified Permissions now supports policy store aliases and named policies and policy templates

external.description:
🚀 Stop Shaming, Start Starting: The Real Reason You're Procrastinating

Ever wondered why you’re staring at your screen for hours, paralyzed by a to-do list that feels like a death sentence? Hint: It is not because you are lazy.

In this episode, we dive deep into the neuroscience of procrastination to reveal a controversial truth: Procrastination is an emotional regulation strategy, not a character flaw. We explore the Amygdala Hijack, explaining how your brain perceives a simple task as a biological threat, triggering a freeze response at work that leaves you stuck in a soul-crushing self-shame cycle.

🧠 Why You Can't "Just Do It"

Most productivity hacks fail because they ignore your biology. We break down the shift from executive dysfunction vs. laziness and why high-achievers often fall into productivity paralysis. You'll learn:
- The Science of Avoidance: How negative self-labeling fuels the brain's threat response.
- The Dopamine Hack: Why task micro-slicing and celebrating minor wins is the only way to build neural momentum.
- Path of Least Resistance: Moving from perfectionism to consistency using micro-habits for overwhelm.
❓ Frequently Asked Questions 

Q: Why am I so motivated until I actually have to start?
A: This is often an Amygdala Hijack. Your brain views the magnitude of the task as a threat to your safety, triggering avoidance to lower immediate cortisol levels.

Q: Is procrastination a trauma response?
A: It can be. For many, the freeze response is a survival mechanism triggered by the fear of failure or the weight of high expectations.

Q: How do I stop feeling ashamed for being unproductive?
A: By practicing affect labeling. Naming the resistance as "overwhelm" rather than "laziness" lowers the emotional charge and allows for compassion-focused productivity.

Stop fighting your biology and start working with it. It’s time to trade the shame for a self-compassion loop that actually gets things done.

✨ Ready to break the cycle? Subscribe now and leave a review if this reframed your brain!

#MicroHabits #Neuroscience #ProductivityParalysis #SelfCompassion
external.title: You’re Not Lazy: The Brain Hack to Stop Procrastinating for Good

external.description:
🧬 The 50-Year Mystery Is Over: Did AI Just Unlock the Cheat Code for Life? 🧬

What if I told you that the secret to curing every disease on Earth was hidden inside a puzzle we couldn’t solve for over half a century? For 50 years, the world’s most brilliant scientists were stumped by a single, mind-bending riddle: The Protein Folding Problem. It was the ultimate biological wall—until DeepMind’s AlphaFold 2 showed up and smashed through it like a digital sledgehammer.

In this episode, we’re diving into the heart of the artificial intelligence revolution. We explore how AlphaFold 2 didn’t just guess the answers—it decoded the very biological blueprints of existence. We’re talking about a shift so massive it makes the discovery of DNA look like a warm-up act. Is it controversial to say machines now understand biology better than humans? Maybe. But the results speak for themselves.

Inside this deep dive, we uncover:
- 🚀 Beyond Human Intuition: How machine learning solved a 50-year-old puzzle that baffled Nobel laureates.
- 💊 The End of 'Incurable': How AI is hyper-accelerating drug discovery for Alzheimer’s, Cancer, and malaria.
- 🌍 Engineering the Future: Why this technology is the secret weapon for climate science and revolutionary material engineering.
- 🔥 Exponential Progress: Why we have entered a new era where the 'impossible' is now just a manageable task.
This isn't just about code and chemistry; it’s about the structural biology of you. We’re witnessing a moment where AI in healthcare moves from science fiction to the silent engine of human survival. Whether you're a tech enthusiast, a science nerd, or just curious about the future of the human race, this episode is a roadmap to the exponential technology reshaping our world.

Don't get left behind in the old world.

🎧 Listen now to see how the code of life has finally been cracked!

If this episode blew your mind, share it with one friend who needs to hear about the future, and hit that subscribe button to stay ahead of the curve! Let’s decode the future together. ✨

images.alt:
A small black French bulldog sits centered atop a narrow, weathered pedestal, its compact body tense but still. The dog’s fur is glossy and textured, catching soft light that highlights the folds of its face and the subtle sheen along its shoulders. Its expression is slightly annoyed or resigned, with one eye tilted upward as if tracking the precarious object above it. Balanced carefully on the dog’s head is a single bright yellow lemon, its textured rind sharply detailed and contrasting against the dark fur.

The pedestal beneath the dog appears aged and worn, with pale cream-colored material that looks partially melted or dripping over the edges, forming slow, viscous streaks down its sides. Dark ivy vines wrap around the base and spill outward, their leaves curling and slightly wilted, adding a sense of quiet decay.

The background is an abstract blend of muted greens and soft beige tones, painted in layered brushstrokes that feel both textured and atmospheric. The space is undefined, neither indoors nor outdoors, giving the scene a suspended, dreamlike quality. The composition feels balanced yet uneasy, as if the smallest shift could break the fragile stillness.

Keywords:
surreal dog, french bulldog, lemon balance, pedestal, ivy vines, dripping texture, painterly background, muted green tones, odd composition, whimsical realism

images.alt:
A tight, centered portrait of a young woman’s face is partially concealed behind a dense curtain of vertical strands that resemble metallic threads or fine tinsel. The strands hang straight down from above, filling the entire frame from edge to edge, creating a layered barrier between viewer and subject. They shimmer subtly with muted iridescence, catching hints of green, copper, and dull silver as light grazes their surfaces. Through narrow gaps, her face becomes visible in fragments, most prominently her eyes, which are sharply focused and stare directly forward with an intense, unsettling calm.

Her skin appears pale with natural texture, including faint freckles across the nose and cheeks. Dark shadows encircle her eyes, giving a hollowed, sleepless look. Her lips are slightly parted, relaxed but unreadable, neither smiling nor frowning. Strands overlap unevenly across her features, slicing her face into thin vertical sections, distorting the sense of continuity and making her expression feel fractured.

The lighting is low and directional, emphasizing contrast. Highlights cling to the threads while her face emerges from shadow behind them, creating depth and a sense of separation. The background is indistinguishable, swallowed by darkness, making the strands feel suspended in space. The overall atmosphere is claustrophobic and intimate, as if the viewer is standing inches away from something watching back, separated only by a fragile, shimmering veil.

Keywords:
veil, portrait, obscured face, vertical lines, haunting gaze, iridescent strands, shadowed eyes, claustrophobic framing, surreal barrier, close-up intensity

AI-generated; use is permitted for non-commercial creative projects. MidJourney, FLUX.1-schnell model via Perchance, Artspace.ai, REVE, Echoform™.

images.alt:
A single, worn armchair upholstered in deep red velvet sits alone in the middle of a vast, cracked desert landscape. The ground stretches endlessly in all directions, fractured into irregular plates of pale, dry earth with dark seams between them, suggesting extreme drought or long abandonment. The chair appears out of place, as if taken from a cozy living room and dropped into this harsh environment. Its fabric is slightly rumpled, with soft shadows emphasizing its plush texture, contrasting sharply against the brittle terrain.

To the right of the chair stands a small, dark wooden side table with curved legs. On it rests a delicate teacup and saucer, faint steam rising from the cup, along with a small vintage clock. The presence of steam suggests warmth and recent activity, adding a subtle tension between comfort and isolation.

The lighting is warm and low, likely from a setting sun, casting long shadows behind the chair and table. The horizon line is distant and slightly blurred, with faint silhouettes of low hills or mountains barely visible. The sky above is muted and hazy, with no distinct clouds, creating a subdued, almost dreamlike atmosphere.

The entire scene feels still and suspended, as if time has slowed or stopped, leaving behind a fragment of domestic life in a place where it does not belong.

Keywords:
desert, armchair, surreal landscape, isolation, cracked earth, abandoned setting, vintage furniture, quiet scene, minimalism, contrast

AI-generated; use is permitted for non-commercial creative projects. MidJourney, FLUX.1-schnell model via Perchance, Artspace.ai, REVE, Echoform™.

National Tartan Day

images.alt:
National Tartan Day is a celebration of Scottish heritage that is observed annually on April 6th in the United States, Canada, and other countries around the world. The day commemorates the signing of the Declaration of Arbroath in 1320, which asserted Scotland's independence from England and paved the way for the country's tuture as a sovereign nation.
Tartan is a traditional Scottish fabric that is woven with a specific pattern of stripes and colors to represent different clans, families, and regions. The wearing of tartan has a long and rich history in Scotland, and it continues to be an important symbol of Scottish identity and culture today.
On National Tartan Day, people of Scottish descent and their friends and allies celebrate their heritage and culture by wearing tartan clothing and accessories, attending Scottish festivals and events, and participating in traditional Scottish activities such as Highland games and ceilidhs.
In the United States, National Tartan Day was officially recognized by Congress in 1998, and it has since become a popular and widely celebrated observance for people of Scottish descent and others who are interested in Scottish culture and history.
